message:
fix: four more bugs from round-4 audit (incl. two undefined-call bugs)

8) install_gen.sh: regex+substring grep for SHA256SUMS

   The generated installer's checksum verifier did:
     expected_sha=$(echo "$checksums" | grep "$filename" | awk '{print $1}')

   Two bugs in one line:
   * `grep "$filename"` treats the filename as a regex — the dot in
     `bv.tar.gz` matches any single char, so the hash for `bvXtarYgz`
     would be accepted.
   * Substring matching means `bv.tar.gz` also matches `prebv.tar.gz`
     and `bv.tar.gz.minisig`. Multiple matches concatenate into a
     multi-line `expected_sha`; the equality check then fails against
     the single-line `actual_sha` and the install aborts with the
     misleading message "checksum mismatch" — even though both
     hashes were actually correct.

   Fix: use awk with literal field-equality on $2, accepting both the
   `<hash>  <name>` (text) and `<hash> *<name>` (binary) sha256sum
   conventions. Verified: returns the exact match, no false positives
   from neighbor filenames.

9) host_health.sh: clock-drift check aborted on non-numeric remote time

   `_hh_check_clock_drift` ran arithmetic directly on the SSH/PowerShell
   output:
     drift_seconds=$((remote_time - local_time))

   With `set -uo pipefail`, a non-numeric value (an SSH MOTD that
   leaked through, a PowerShell warning banner, a partial line, etc.)
   aborts the function with a "syntax error" and the host gets no
   JSON on stdout — the caller treats it as silently broken.

   Fix: validate $remote_time matches ^[0-9]+$ before the subtraction.
   On mismatch, emit a structured `{status: "error", error: "Non-numeric
   remote time: <truncated>"}` JSON via jq for proper escaping.

10) dsr cmd_fallback: called nonexistent `quality_run_checks`

    Step 1 of the fallback pipeline did:
      if quality_run_checks "$tool_name" --work-dir "$local_path" 2>/dev/null; then

    No such function exists in any module — the actual implementation
    is `qg_run_checks` in src/quality_gates.sh. The 2>/dev/null hid
    the `command not found` message and the if-branch always fell to
    the else: every `dsr fallback` invocation that didn't pass
    `--skip-checks` aborted at "Quality checks failed" with no
    actionable diagnostic.

    Fix: call the real `qg_run_checks`. Drop the 2>/dev/null so any
    future "command not found" surfaces immediately rather than
    silently mis-routing the pipeline.

11) dsr cmd_fallback: called nonexistent `signing_is_enabled` and
    `signing_sign_files`

    Step 3 of the fallback pipeline did:
      if signing_is_enabled 2>/dev/null; then
          log_info "Signing artifacts..."
          signing_sign_files "$output_dir" "${tool_name}*" 2>/dev/null || ...

    Neither function existed in src/signing.sh. With 2>/dev/null
    silencing the `command not found`, the if was always false, the
    block was always skipped, and the fallback pipeline NEVER signed
    any release artifact — the documented step 3 was effectively a
    no-op for every dsr release that ever ran through `cmd_fallback`.

    Fix: implement both helpers in src/signing.sh:
    * signing_is_enabled honors DSR_NO_SIGN, DSR_SIGNING_ENABLED, the
      DSR_CONFIG[signing_enabled] config key, and finally the
      keypair-presence gate via signing_check.
    * signing_sign_files takes (dir, glob), expands the glob with
      shopt -s nullglob, skips signature/checksum/SBOM/provenance
      files (so we don't try to sign sigs), and forwards the resulting
      file list to the existing signing_sign_batch.
    Both exported alongside the rest of signing.sh's public API.

Verification:
  * Functional repros for the SHA256 mismatch and the signing helpers
    confirm the new behavior end-to-end.
  * tests/unit/ bats: 290/290 pass.
  * scripts/tests/test_config.sh: 45/45 pass.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -4669,7 +4669,16 @@ EOF
     # === Step 1: Pre-release checks ===
     if ! $skip_checks; then
         log_info "=== Step 1/4: Pre-release Checks ==="
-        if quality_run_checks "$tool_name" --work-dir "$local_path" 2>/dev/null; then
+        # Bug: this previously called `quality_run_checks` — a name that
+        # only exists in the original bd-36r design doc, never in any
+        # shipped module. The real function is `qg_run_checks` (defined
+        # in src/quality_gates.sh). The 2>/dev/null on the call hid the
+        # `command not found` message, so the if branch always fell to
+        # the else: every `dsr fallback` run that didn't pass
+        # --skip-checks aborted at "Quality checks failed" with no
+        # diagnostic path forward. Calling the correct function makes
+        # the pipeline actually executable.
+        if qg_run_checks "$tool_name" --work-dir "$local_path"; then
             checks_status="passed"
             log_ok "Quality checks passed"
         else

@@ -554,6 +554,23 @@ _hh_check_clock_drift() {
         return 1
     fi
 
+    # Validate the remote time is a positive integer before doing
+    # arithmetic on it. The earlier code went straight into
+    # `$((remote_time - local_time))`, which under `set -uo pipefail`
+    # aborts the function with a "syntax error" the moment the SSH/
+    # PowerShell call returns something non-numeric (a PowerShell warn
+    # banner, a partial line, an SSH MOTD that leaked through, etc.) —
+    # the caller would then see the function exit with no JSON on
+    # stdout and treat the host as silently broken. Use jq to safely
+    # encode the (possibly multi-line / quote-containing) error value.
+    if [[ ! "$remote_time" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
+        local _hh_truncated="${remote_time:0:200}"
+        local _hh_err
+        _hh_err=$(jq -nc --arg msg "Non-numeric remote time: $_hh_truncated" '{drift_seconds: null, status: "error", error: $msg}')
+        echo "$_hh_err"
+        return 1
+    fi
+
     drift_seconds=$((remote_time - local_time))
     [[ $drift_seconds -lt 0 ]] && drift_seconds=$((-drift_seconds))

@@ -456,7 +456,21 @@ _download_and_verify() {
             local expected_sha
             local filename
             filename=$(basename "$dest")
-            expected_sha=$(echo "$checksums" | grep "$filename" | awk '{print $1}')
+            # Match the EXACT filename. Earlier this used
+            #   grep "$filename" | awk '{print $1}'
+            # which (a) treated $filename as a regex — so the dot in
+            # `bv.tar.gz` matched any single char and would happily
+            # accept a hash for `bvXtarYgz`, and (b) used substring
+            # matching, so `bv.tar.gz` also matched `prebv.tar.gz` or
+            # `bv.tar.gz.minisig`. Either path could feed the wrong
+            # hash into the verifier — silently downgrading the
+            # integrity check or outright accepting a tampered asset.
+            # Use awk with literal field equality on the second column,
+            # accepting both the `<hash>  <name>` (text) and
+            # `<hash> *<name>` (binary) sha256sum conventions.
+            expected_sha=$(printf '%s\n' "$checksums" | awk -v fname="$filename" '
+                $2 == fname || $2 == "*" fname {print $1; exit}
+            ')
 
             if [[ -n "$expected_sha" ]]; then
                 local actual_sha

@@ -463,6 +463,74 @@ signing_sign_batch() {
     return 0
 }
 
+# Predicate used by orchestrators to decide whether to call into the
+# signing pipeline. Honors:
+#   - DSR_NO_SIGN=1 / DSR_SIGNING_ENABLED=false to disable explicitly
+#   - signing.enabled in DSR_CONFIG (loaded by config.sh) when present
+#   - the presence of a usable keypair (signing_check) as the final gate
+# Returns 0 if signing should run, 1 otherwise.
+# Usage: signing_is_enabled
+signing_is_enabled() {
+    # Explicit env opt-out wins.
+    if [[ "${DSR_NO_SIGN:-}" == "1" ]] || [[ "${DSR_NO_SIGN:-}" == "true" ]]; then
+        return 1
+    fi
+    if [[ "${DSR_SIGNING_ENABLED:-}" == "false" ]] || [[ "${DSR_SIGNING_ENABLED:-}" == "0" ]]; then
+        return 1
+    fi
+    # config.sh loaded? Honor signing_enabled in the in-memory config.
+    if declare -p DSR_CONFIG &>/dev/null; then
+        local cfg_value="${DSR_CONFIG[signing_enabled]:-true}"
+        case "$cfg_value" in
+            false|0|no|off) return 1 ;;
+        esac
+    fi
+    # Final gate: only enabled if the keypair actually exists and is
+    # usable. signing_check writes diagnostic logs to stderr; suppress
+    # those here because callers use this as a "should I bother?" check.
+    signing_check >/dev/null 2>&1
+}
+
+# Sign every file in a directory matching a glob pattern. Convenience
+# wrapper that expands the pattern (skipping already-signed sigs and
+# obvious non-artifacts like checksums and SBOMs) and forwards to
+# signing_sign_batch.
+# Usage: signing_sign_files <dir> <glob_pattern>
+signing_sign_files() {
+    local dir="${1:-}"
+    local pattern="${2:-*}"
+
+    if [[ -z "$dir" || ! -d "$dir" ]]; then
+        _sign_log_error "signing_sign_files: directory not found: $dir"
+        return 4
+    fi
+
+    # Build the list of candidates with explicit globbing so the caller
+    # doesn't have to. Skip files that wouldn't make sense to sign:
+    # checksums, SBOMs, provenance, existing signatures.
+    local -a files=()
+    local f
+    shopt -s nullglob
+    for f in "$dir"/$pattern; do
+        [[ -f "$f" ]] || continue
+        case "$(basename "$f")" in
+            *.minisig|*.sig|*.asc) continue ;;
+            *SHA256*|*sha256*|*checksums*) continue ;;
+            *.sbom.*|*.intoto.jsonl) continue ;;
+        esac
+        files+=("$f")
+    done
+    shopt -u nullglob
+
+    if [[ ${#files[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then
+        _sign_log_warn "signing_sign_files: no files match $dir/$pattern"
+        return 0
+    fi
+
+    signing_sign_batch "${files[@]}"
+}
+
 # Export functions for use by other scripts
 export -f signing_require_minisign signing_check signing_init signing_fix_permissions
 export -f signing_sign signing_verify signing_get_public_key signing_sign_batch
+export -f signing_is_enabled signing_sign_files
